title: Mathematical Weakness Audit of Current Attention Mechanisms
description: Conduct a systematic audit of specific mathematical weaknesses and inefficiencies across all attention families surveyed in the research proposal. For FlashAttention: identify the precise FLOP/byte bottleneck of softmax vs tensor cores (the ~256× throughput gap), the asymmetric scaling trap where each GPU generation requires kernel redesign, and the mathematical cost of recomputation-based backward passes. For RoPE: formalize the frequency extrapolation failure mode (unseen rotation angles in high-frequency dimensions), the attention entropy collapse phenomenon (softmax entropy → log(N)), and embedding collapse toward low-frequency components. For SSMs: characterize the 'fuzzy memory' limitation for exact token-level retrieval, the state expansion vs quality tradeoff, and the lack of a native mechanism for content-addressable lookup. For linear attention: analyze approximation error bounds and the rank bottleneck in kernelized formulations. Quantify each weakness with concrete numbers, equations, and failure modes. Output: a ranked catalog of weaknesses by severity and tractability, with formal mathematical statements of each.

title: Novel Attention Formulation: Mathematical Definition and Core Mechanism
description: Based on the weakness audit (sub-topic 1), design a genuinely novel attention mechanism. Give it a distinctive name. Define it mathematically with full equations: the forward pass formulation, backward pass gradients, and all intermediate computations. The formulation must address the softmax bottleneck (e.g., replacing or restructuring the nonlinearity to avoid the exponential function unit bottleneck), maintain or improve retrieval quality (unlike SSMs' fuzzy memory), and achieve superior complexity. Key design goals: (a) the nonlinear normalization should be expressible as a sequence of matrix multiplies (not element-wise exponentials), leveraging tensor cores for the entire forward pass; (b) the mechanism should admit a dual form — quadratic for training, linear/recurrent for inference — mathematically proven; (c) it should preserve the relative-position encoding property of RoPE. Define all symbols, dimensions, and hyperparameters. State the key theorem: what complexity class, what error guarantees, what memory bound. The mechanism must be genuinely novel — not just recombination of FlashAttention tiling + linear attention — but something like a new normalization function, a new structured sparsity pattern motivated by information theory, or a new hybrid compute paradigm.

title: Complexity Analysis, Error Bounds, and Theoretical Guarantees
description: Provide rigorous complexity analysis and theoretical guarantees for the proposed mechanism (sub-topic 2). Derive: (a) computational complexity — FLOPs as a function of sequence length N, head dimension d, and block size B; compare O(N²d) standard vs O(N²d²/M) FlashAttention vs your mechanism; (b) memory complexity — HBM reads/writes, SRAM usage, and whether the mechanism is IO-optimal under the roofline model; (c) approximation error bounds — if your mechanism approximates softmax attention, prove an error bound (e.g., ||Output_yours - Output_softmax|| ≤ ε) under stated assumptions; (d) numerical stability analysis — prove bounds on floating-point error accumulation, especially for the backward pass; (e) convergence guarantee — prove or argue that the mechanism, when used as a drop-in replacement, does not degrade training convergence. If the mechanism is exact (like FlashAttention), prove bitwise equivalence. If it's approximate, prove a contraction or Lipschitz property. Use formal theorem/proof style.

title: Hardware-Aware Algorithm Design: Tiling, Memory Hierarchy, and Kernel Architecture
description: Design the hardware-aware implementation strategy for the proposed mechanism (sub-topic 2). Specify: (a) Tiling strategy — how Q, K, V are partitioned into blocks for SRAM; block sizes B_r, B_c as functions of d, M (SRAM capacity), and the mechanism's specific compute pattern; (b) Memory hierarchy — what lives in HBM vs SRAM vs registers vs TMEM (for Blackwell-class GPUs); when data is loaded, stored, and recomputed; (c) Kernel design — pseudocode for the forward CUDA kernel showing warp scheduling, barrier synchronization, and how the mechanism's nonlinearity is mapped to either tensor cores (if expressible as matmuls) or FMA/EX2 units; (d) Asynchronous pipelining — how to overlap data movement (TMA on Hopper/Blackwell) with computation using warp specialization; (e) Backward pass strategy — what intermediate values to store vs recompute, and how the mechanism's gradient computation differs from standard attention; (f) FP8/FP4 compatibility — whether the mechanism admits low-precision computation with incoherent processing. Address the asymmetric scaling issue explicitly: does your mechanism avoid reliance on MUFU.EX2 units? If so, how? If not, how do you mitigate the bottleneck?

title: Attention Entropy, Length Generalization, and Positional Encoding Integration
description: Analyze how the proposed mechanism (sub-topic 2) interacts with attention entropy, length generalization, and positional encodings. Building on the survey's finding that attention entropy control is the unifying framework for length generalization: (a) Derive the entropy of your mechanism's attention distribution as a function of sequence length N — does it exhibit the same entropy → log(N) collapse as softmax, or does it maintain stable entropy? (b) If the mechanism uses a different normalization than softmax, analyze its asymptotic entropy behavior and prove whether it avoids the 'attention dilution' problem. (c) Design the integration with RoPE or propose a replacement positional encoding that is mathematically compatible with your mechanism — prove that the relative-position property (q_m^T R_{n-m} k_n) is preserved or show what property replaces it. (d) Analyze length extrapolation: can your mechanism be used at sequence lengths far beyond training without catastrophic degradation? If so, prove why. If not, specify the fine-tuning or calibration needed. (e) Address the NoPE challenge — can your mechanism function without explicit position encoding, and does it avoid the 'attention distribution distraction' failure mode identified by Wang et al.?

title: Quantitative Comparison Against FlashAttention v4, Standard Attention, and SSM/Linear Alternatives
description: Construct a rigorous quantitative comparison of the proposed mechanism against: FlashAttention v4 (B200 at 1,613 TFLOPS/s), standard PyTorch attention (as baseline), Mamba-2/SSD (O(N) recurrence mode), linear attention (kernelized), and hybrid Mamba-2-Hybrid. Produce a comparison table with these columns: Mechanism, FLOPs (forward + backward as function of N, d), HBM reads/writes (bytes), SRAM usage (bytes), Arithmetic intensity (FLOP/byte), Expected throughput (TFLOPS/s on B200-class hardware), Memory for KV cache (bytes as function of N), Retrieval quality (qualitative: exact/approximate/limited), Length generalization (qualitative: what N can be supported with training at N₀), Numerical precision support (FP32/FP16/BF16/FP8/FP4). For expected speedup: use the roofline model with B200 parameters (2.25 PFLOPS tensor core peak, ~4 TB/s HBM bandwidth) to calculate the compute-bound vs memory-bound crossover point for each mechanism. Show a speedup graph (as a table of N, d values) indicating where your mechanism dominates. Be conservative — acknowledge where FlashAttention v4 or SSMs may still be superior (e.g., very short sequences, pure inference with batch=1).

title: Implementation Roadmap, Pseudo-Code, and Experimental Validation Design
description: Synthesize all prior sub-topics into: (a) Complete algorithmic pseudo-code for the forward and backward passes — written at the level of detail that could be directly translated to CUDA/Triton. Include the tiling loops, online statistics maintenance, the new normalization kernel, and the backward recomputation strategy. (b) Implementation roadmap: what existing library to build on (CUTLASS, Triton, or from scratch), estimated engineering effort per phase, and which hardware targets (A100, H100, B200) to support first. (c) Experimental validation design — propose a set of experiments to validate the theoretical claims: perplexity on OpenWebText/C4 vs baseline Transformer at matched FLOPs; retrieval accuracy on Needle-in-a-Haystack at 32K/128K/256K; training throughput (tokens/sec) on a single B200 at various sequence lengths; scaling law experiment (loss vs FLOPs) to verify the mechanism maintains Transformer scaling properties; ablation study isolating each component (new normalization, tiling strategy, positional encoding). (d) Potential failure modes and limitations: what could go wrong in practice that theory doesn't capture, and how to detect and mitigate these. (e) Path to production: what would be needed to integrate this mechanism into PyTorch (nn.MultiheadAttention replacement), HuggingFace Transformers, and vLLM for inference.
